expectedPeriodInfo.title: Menstruation
expectedPeriodInfo.desc: This date is calculated based on your history. A shift of 2–3 days is a normal body reaction to stress, sleep, or climate change.
expectedPeriodInfo.tip: Tip: To make the forecast even more accurate, log your symptoms and stress levels. This helps the algorithm better understand your personal rhythms.

phase1.title: Preparation (Follicular Phase)
phase1.desc: Your body begins preparing for a new cycle. FSH levels stimulate follicular growth, gradually raising your estrogen and laying the foundation for future ovulation.
phase1.tip: Tip: Start daily BBT measurements at the same time. This creates a "baseline" so the algorithm can accurately detect when the egg is released.

phase2.title: Waiting (Fertile Window)
phase2.desc: Estrogen levels are rising, making the environment highly favorable for sperm. Your "golden window" is open—chances for successful conception are increasing.
phase2.tip: Tip: Start taking LH tests twice a day. A peak hormone concentration tells the algorithm ovulation will occur within 24–48 hours.

phase3.title: Ovulation (Peak Day)
phase3.desc: Your biological peak. LH levels have maxed out, triggering egg release. This is the most effective moment in your cycle for conception.
phase3.tip: Tip: Today is crucial. A positive LH test combined with a confirmed BBT shift allows Eluna to lock your ovulation with 100% precision.

phase4.title: Awaiting Result (Luteal Phase)
phase4.desc: Ovulation is complete, and progesterone levels begin to rise. Your body is now creating conditions for potential implantation and maintaining internal balance.
phase4.tip: Tip: Focus on rest and quality sleep. Continue logging symptom data: this helps the algorithm understand if pregnancy has occurred.

averageCycleInfo.title: Average Cycle
averageCycleInfo.desc: This is the average length of your cycle based on recent months. This metric helps the algorithm map your body's rhythms and forecast dates with up to 95% accuracy.
averageCycleInfo.tip: Tip: Keep logging your cycle start dates. The more historical data available, the better Eluna adapts to your individual patterns and the more accurately it predicts your fertile window.

conceptionInfo.title: Conception Chance
conceptionInfo.descHigh: High: You are in your fertile window. These are the most favorable days for planning a pregnancy.
conceptionInfo.descMedium: Medium: Your fertile window is approaching or has recently ended. The probability of pregnancy is moderate.
conceptionInfo.descLow: Low: In the current phase of your cycle, the probability of pregnancy is minimal.
conceptionInfo.howItWorksDesc:
• The algorithm analyzes your cycles and biomarkers (BBT, LH, mucus)
• Calculates the fertile window (up to 6 days)
• Maximum probability is on the day of ovulation and the day before

confidenceInfo.title: Forecast Accuracy
confidenceInfo.descHigh: High confidence: The algorithm has gathered enough biomarker data for a precise mathematical forecast.
confidenceInfo.descMedium: Medium confidence: Forecast is largely based on historical averages. Add more LH tests or BBT to personalize.
confidenceInfo.descLow: Low confidence: The system lacks data about your cycle. Regularly log your period and symptoms to improve accuracy.
confidenceInfo.howToImproveDesc:
• Regularly log your period dates
• Take ovulation (LH) tests
• Measure and log Basal Body Temperature (BBT)

Ovulation.tip: Egg is released. Conception chance is dropping.

LutealLate.tip: PMS is possible. Take care of yourself.

activeSearch.title: Waiting for period
activeSearch.desc: Your cycle is lasting longer than usual. This may be related to stress, late ovulation, or other factors.

clinicalDelay.title: Unusually long cycle
clinicalDelay.desc: Your cycle has been going on for {{days}} days. If this is not typical for you, we recommend taking a test or consulting a specialist.

amenorrhea.title: Period absent for 30+ days
amenorrhea.desc: Absence of menstruation for more than 30 days may be a sign of amenorrhea. Eluna recommends consulting a doctor.

menstruation.desc1: Low estrogen weakens skin barrier functions. Dryness and reactivity are possible.
menstruation.tip1: Tip: Use gentle cleansing and basic hydration.
menstruation.desc2: The body spends resources on endometrial renewal. Serotonin levels are temporarily reduced.
menstruation.tip2: Tip: A full night's sleep and walks will help you recover faster.
menstruation.desc3: Prostaglandins can cause contractions and lower back discomfort.
menstruation.tip3: Tip: A heating pad or warm bath will help relax smooth muscles.

follicular.desc1: Rising estrogen stimulates collagen and hyaluronic acid production. Skin becomes firmer.
follicular.tip1: Tip: Ideal time to introduce new active ingredients to your routine.
follicular.desc2: Hormones boost cognitive functions and stamina. You feel a rush of energy.
follicular.tip2: Tip: Plan your most complex tasks and active workouts for these days.
follicular.desc3: Dopamine levels rise, making you more sociable and confident.
follicular.tip3: Tip: Great time for networking and public speaking.

ovulatory.desc1: Estrogen is at its peak, pores are minimized, and facial tone is most even. You look rested.
ovulatory.tip1: Tip: Minimal makeup will highlight your natural beauty.
ovulatory.desc2: Peak testosterone and estrogen deliver maximum physical strength and libido.
ovulatory.tip2: Tip: Use this drive to achieve your most ambitious goals.
ovulatory.desc3: Your body signals readiness for conception by increasing fertile signs.
ovulatory.tip3: Tip: Listen to your body's signals; you are highly sensitive right now.

luteal.desc1: Progesterone stimulates sebaceous glands, which can lead to breakouts.
luteal.tip1: Tip: Focus on quality cleansing and detox masks.
luteal.desc2: The body retains sodium, causing mild swelling and heaviness.
luteal.tip2: Tip: Reduce salt intake and add light lymphatic drainage.
luteal.desc3: The pre-cycle hormone drop can cause irritability (PMS).
luteal.tip3: Tip: Practice meditation and create the most comfortable environment around you.

menstruation.title: Silence and Recovery
menstruation.desc: Time to slow down and listen to yourself. Your body is renewing, directing all resources toward recovery and rest.
menstruation.skin: Sensitivity is elevated. Dryness and dullness are possible. Gentle hydration and avoiding aggressive peels are recommended.
menstruation.energy: Low hormone levels. Natural decline in physical strength and focus. Ideal time for full sleep and calm walks.
menstruation.body: Cleansing processes are underway. Discomfort in the lower back and lower abdomen is possible. Gentle warmth and rest will help relax the muscles.

follicular.title: Rise and Creation
follicular.desc: Energy returns along with rising estrogen. The perfect moment for new beginnings, active plans, and confident forward movement.
follicular.skin: Skin condition is improving rapidly. Ideal time for new cosmetic procedures, light peeling or cleansing.
follicular.energy: Estrogen is rising. Stamina is recovering. Great time for intense workouts and cardio.
follicular.body: Your body feels light. Flexibility and coordination improve, muscles respond faster to stress.

ovulatory.title: Peak and Radiance
ovulatory.desc: Your biological maximum. You are at the peak of attraction and confidence—the perfect time to shine and transmit your energy to the world.
ovulatory.skin: Your skin looks glowing, smooth, and clear. A simple basic skincare routine is all you need right now.
ovulatory.energy: Peak physical and social energy. Plan important meetings, dates, and ambitious projects.
ovulatory.body: Libido is high. You are at your most attractive, your body feels strong and resilient.

luteal.title: Coziness and Care
luteal.desc: A period of gentle slowing down and internal focus. Surround yourself with comfort, reduce the pace, and be as kind to yourself as possible.
luteal.skin: Possible increased skin oiliness and breakouts. Cleanse gently, avoid aggressive scrubs.
luteal.energy: Estrogen drops, irritability is possible. Try to get more sleep and postpone high-energy tasks.
luteal.body: Progesterone is rising. Possible fluid retention, breast tenderness, and mild bloating. Be kind to yourself.

calendarScreen.anomalyGenericDesc: You entered data that falls outside the normal cycle. Are you sure you want to save it as an anomaly?
calendarScreen.anomalyCycleLongDesc: You specified a cycle length of {{cLength}} days. This significantly exceeds the norm. Did you perhaps forget to log your previous period? If you are sure everything is correct, you can save this data as an anomaly.
calendarScreen.anomalyCycleShortDesc: You specified a cycle length of only {{cLength}} days. This falls outside normal physiology. Are you sure you want to save it as an anomaly?
calendarScreen.anomalyMensesDesc: You specified a period length of {{mLength}} days. Usually, it does not exceed 7-8 days. Ensure you didn't accidentally highlight extra days.
calendarScreen.anomalousMensesAlert: This period appears to be longer than normal (over 14 days). To maintain prediction accuracy, we only consider the first 14 days.
calendarScreen.anomalyCycleWarning: This cycle looks unusual and is excluded from mathematical predictions. Please check your period dates.

addLogModal.title: Add a log
addLogModal.notesPlaceholder: Describe your symptoms or thoughts (up to 1000 characters)...

insights.day1: Cycle starts. Progesterone drops, causing endometrial shedding and possible cramps.
insights.day2: Peak flow. You may experience increased fatigue and sleepiness due to iron loss.
insights.day3: Body adapts. Inflammatory processes subside, and cramps become weaker.
insights.day4: Estrogen begins a slow rise. Mood stabilizes, and skin becomes clearer.
insights.day5: Phase ending. Energy returns as the body prepares for a new egg maturation.
insights.day6_8: Estrogen rises. Notice an increase in focus and social activity.
insights.day9_10: Skin glows. Hormones stimulate collagen production, and libido begins to rise.
insights.day11_12: Fertility window opens. Discharge becomes wetter ('creamy' or 'watery'), conception chance rises.
insights.day13: Pre-ovulatory peak. Maximum energy and confidence levels before the egg is released.
insights.day14: Ovulation. The egg is released. Mild pulling pain (mittelschmerz) and peak libido possible.
insights.day15: Golden window closes. Basal body temperature begins to rise, confirming ovulation completion.
insights.day16_18: Progesterone takes over. You may feel calm and a slight decrease in social activity.
insights.day19_21: Implantation window. The body is in standby mode, metabolism slightly accelerates.
insights.day22_24: Onset of PMS. Possible breast tenderness, bloating, and mild irritability.
insights.day25_26: Emotional background is unstable. Hormone drops can cause anxiety or sadness.
insights.day27_28: Standby mode. The body prepares for a new cycle. Reduce workouts and ensure quality sleep.

medicalExplanation.follicularNormal: During the follicular phase, estrogen levels begin to rise, which often correlates with increased energy and improved mood. This is your body preparing for ovulation.

patterns.historicalMatchSandwich: We analyzed your data and noticed that you experienced the same thing during past cycles in this phase. This may be related to your individual response to hormonal shifts.

recovery.title: Record Analysis
recovery.severeDescription: These data have an anomalous duration. Often such records appear if you accidentally forgot to log one of your periods, causing the cycle to stretch. To avoid false predictions, these cycles are temporarily excluded from Eluna's analytics.
recovery.highAnomalyDesc: More than 80% of your records fall outside the normal cycle. We recommend double-checking the dates, as this significantly reduces prediction accuracy.
recovery.warningDesc: Many of your cycles are longer or shorter than average. They are included in analytics, but reduce the algorithm's overall confidence.
recovery.autoFixDesc: We will trim the duration of anomalous periods to 14 days to restore analytics. This action can be undone at any time.

anomaly.title: Anomalous Data
anomaly.desc: Analytics are based on {{used}} out of {{total}} cycles. {{excluded}} cycle(s) were excluded from predictions as anomalous.

cyclePhases.tooltip: Icons show your cycle phase. Tap a day to see what it means.
cyclePhases.descMenstruation: Shedding of the uterine lining. Energy levels may be lower.
cyclePhases.descFollicular: Estrogen rises. A time of increasing energy and preparation for ovulation.
cyclePhases.descFertileWindow: The days leading up to and including ovulation when pregnancy is possible.
cyclePhases.descOvulation: An egg is released. Peak energy and fertility.
cyclePhases.descLuteal: Progesterone rises. Energy may drop as the body prepares for the next cycle.
cyclePhases.descLutealLate: Hormones drop before menstruation. You may experience PMS symptoms like mood swings or bloating.

about.title: About App
about.description: Eluna is an offline-first, privacy-focused menstrual cycle tracker with an intelligent analytics engine. All your data is encrypted and securely stored only on this device.
about.licensesDesc: We use the following open-source software and components:

privacy.title: 1. Privacy Policy
privacy.subtitle: YOUR DATA IS ONLY YOURS
privacy.p1: We believe that women's health information is the most intimate sphere. Therefore, Eluna is built on the Zero-Knowledge principle:
privacy.p2: Local Storage: All symptoms, dates, and notes you enter are stored exclusively in an encrypted database on your device.
privacy.p3: No Servers: Eluna does not have centralized servers to collect your personal data. We cannot see, sell, or transfer your information to third parties because we do not have access to it.
privacy.p4: Your Responsibility: Since the data is stored on the device, its safety depends on you. If you delete the app without a cloud backup, the data will be irretrievably lost.

eula.title: 2. End User License Agreement (EULA)
eula.subtitle: ELUNA TERMS OF USE
eula.p1: License: Eluna grants you a personal, non-exclusive license to use the app for personal purposes.
eula.p2: Updates: We are constantly improving our forecasting algorithms, so the functionality and interface may be updated.
eula.p3: PRO Subscription: Paid features are provided according to the terms of the selected plan. Subscription management and refunds are handled through the Apple App Store / Google Play Store.
eula.p4: Limitation of Liability: The developer is not responsible for technical failures caused by the operation of your device's operating system.

disclaimer.title: 3. Medical Disclaimer
disclaimer.subtitle: ATTENTION: THIS IS NOT A MEDICAL DEVICE
disclaimer.p1: Eluna is a tool for self-monitoring and visualizing your biological rhythms. The information in the app is for informational purposes only.
disclaimer.p2: Not a Contraceptive: Eluna's predictions (including "Golden Days") cannot be used as a method to prevent pregnancy.
disclaimer.p3: Not a Diagnosis: Eluna does not replace a doctor's consultation. All medical decisions, including taking medications, must be agreed upon with a specialist.
disclaimer.p4: Individuality: The accuracy of predictions depends on the stability of your cycle and the regularity of keeping your diary. Biological cycles can change under the influence of stress, illness, and external factors.
disclaimer.p5: By using Eluna, you confirm that you assume full responsibility for decisions made based on the app's data.

texts.t1: Role of the hypothalamus in the menstrual cycle
texts.t3: The hypothalamus sets the rhythm of the cycle through pulsatile GnRH secretion.
texts.t4: The hypothalamus is the primary regulator of the hypothalamic–pituitary–ovarian axis. It secretes GnRH pulsatilly rather than continuously; it is the pulse pattern that determines FSH and LH secretion.
texts.t5: The role of the hypothalamus in the menstrual cycle is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t6: The hypothalamus synchronizes the cycle via GnRH neurons. They release GnRH in pulses, not continuously. In the follicular phase, the pulse frequency typically increases, while in the luteal phase, it slows down under the influence of progesterone. Disruptions in hypothalamic regulation can lead to anovulation, oligomenorrhea, and functional hypothalamic amenorrhea.
texts.t7: if the cycle has become irregular, too infrequent, or has ceased
texts.t8: if there are signs of anovulation, infertility, or pronounced symptoms of hormonal disorders
texts.t9: if there is rapid progression of symptoms, very heavy bleeding, severe headaches, visual disturbances, or suspicion of an abnormal pregnancy
texts.t16: Values depend on the method, laboratory, age, clinical context, and day of the cycle.
texts.t18: combination of absent periods with weight loss, stress, or excessive exercise
texts.t19: the hormone should be evaluated in the context of the cycle phase, symptoms, and clinical objective
texts.t20: persistent hormone deviation along with symptoms and cycle disruption
texts.t28: role of the hypothalamus in the menstrual cycle
texts.t29: physiology and clinical interpretation can be explained
texts.t30: a diagnosis cannot be made based on a single symptom or a single test without context
texts.t31: recurrent cycle disruptions or pronounced symptoms require evaluation of the cause
texts.t34: if there is amenorrhea/anovulation and low-normal gonadotropins -> consider central suppression of the axis
texts.t35: if stress, energy deficiency, or excessive exercise are present -> evaluate for functional hypothalamic dysfunction
texts.t36: making a diagnosis based on a single test outside of the clinical context
texts.t37: interpreting hormones without considering the cycle phase and ovulation
texts.t39: a symptom without a repeatable pattern across cycles
texts.t40: clarify the physiological role of the hormone or axis
texts.t41: explain how this relates to the phases of the cycle
texts.t42: add clinical interpretation and red flags
texts.t46: The hypothalamus regulates the cycle through pulsatile GnRH secretion.
texts.t47: Disruption of hypothalamic regulation can lead to amenorrhea and anovulation.
texts.t48: Priority was given to clinical guidelines, Endotext/NCBI Bookshelf, and reviews on reproductive endocrinology.
texts.t59: clarify the cycle phase and the presence of ovulation
texts.t60: correlate laboratory data with symptoms and ultrasound/medical history
texts.t61: in case of persistent deviation, look for the primary cause rather than limiting to a description of the result
texts.t62: do not interpret hormones in isolation from clinical presentation
texts.t63: do not use colloquial terms as a substitute for a diagnosis
texts.t64: Hormone interpretation requires consideration of clinical context, cycle phase, and analytical method; universal simplifications are often misleading.
texts.t66: Role of the pituitary gland in the cycle
texts.t68: The pituitary gland translates GnRH signals into FSH and LH secretion.
texts.t69: The anterior pituitary responds to GnRH by releasing FSH and LH, which in turn trigger follicle growth, estradiol synthesis, ovulation, and luteinization.
texts.t70: The role of the pituitary gland in the cycle is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t71: The pituitary gland is the central mediator between the hypothalamus and the ovaries. FSH primarily supports follicle growth and estradiol synthesis in granulosa cells, while LH stimulates theca cells and is involved in ovulation and corpus luteum formation. Changes at the pituitary level can disrupt the ovulatory cycle even when the ovaries are intact.
texts.t75: role of the pituitary gland in the cycle
texts.t78: if prolactin is persistently elevated -> rule out medications, pregnancy, hypothyroidism, and prolactinoma
texts.t79: if galactorrhea, amenorrhea, or infertility is present -> an evaluation of a pituitary cause is needed
texts.t80: The pituitary gland responds to GnRH with FSH and LH secretion.
texts.t81: Pituitary disorders are included in the differential diagnosis of amenorrhea and anovulation.
texts.t86: What is GnRH and why is it needed
texts.t87: GnRH is gonadotropin-releasing hormone, the primary signal from the hypothalamus to the pituitary gland.
texts.t88: GnRH is required for the pituitary gland to secrete FSH and LH. Pulsatile secretion is critical: with continuous administration, pituitary receptors become desensitized.
texts.t89: What is GnRH and why is it needed — a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t90: GnRH is the central neuroendocrine switch of reproduction. Pulsatile secretion maintains the physiological function of the HPO axis; changes in pulse frequency affect the LH and FSH profile during different phases of the cycle. Therefore, in clinical practice, GnRH agonists and antagonists can either stimulate or suppress reproductive function depending on the administration regimen.
texts.t91: what is gnrh and why is it needed
texts.t93: GnRH works the same with continuous and pulsatile administration
texts.t96: GnRH is the primary hypothalamic stimulus for FSH and LH secretion.
texts.t97: Pulsatile GnRH secretion is necessary to maintain normal pituitary function.
texts.t100: FSH stimulates follicle growth and helps select the dominant follicle.
texts.t101: FSH acts primarily on granulosa cells, supporting the growth of antral follicles, aromatase expression, and estradiol synthesis.
texts.t102: FSH is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t103: FSH is particularly important in the early and mid-follicular phase. FSH levels rise during the transition from the luteal phase to the early follicular phase, allowing for the recruitment of a follicle cohort. In clinical practice, FSH is considered part of the ovarian reserve picture but is never interpreted in isolation from age, estradiol, AMH, and the clinical objective.
texts.t106: normal FSH excludes a decrease in ovarian reserve
texts.t109: if the task is to assess ovarian reserve -> interpret markers together, not in isolation
texts.t110: if FSH is elevated and hypoestrogenism is present -> consider decreased ovarian function/POI in the appropriate context
texts.t111: FSH supports follicle growth and estradiol synthesis.
texts.t112: In clinical practice, FSH is not interpreted in isolation, but together with other markers of ovarian reserve.
texts.t116: correlate the marker with age and clinical objective
texts.t117: if necessary, add AFC/ultrasound and other ovarian reserve indicators
texts.t118: do not draw conclusions from a single test out of context
texts.t120: LH is required for final follicle maturation, ovulation, and corpus luteum formation.
texts.t121: In the follicular phase, LH stimulates theca cells, and the preovulatory LH peak triggers the resumption of oocyte meiosis, follicle rupture, and luteinization.
texts.t122: LH is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t123: LH acts not only as a preovulatory trigger. In the first phase of the cycle, it supports the production of androgens in theca cells, which are then converted into estrogens. When high estradiol levels are maintained long enough, an LH surge is formed, triggering ovulation. After this, LH is required for early corpus luteum function.
texts.t127: if there is an LH peak followed by a rise in progesterone/BBT -> ovulation is likely
texts.t128: if bleeding occurs but there are no signs of the luteal phase -> do not consider ovulation proven
texts.t129: a single symptom without confirmation by other signs
texts.t130: bleeding as the sole marker of ovulation
texts.t131: LH is required for preovulatory oocyte maturation and follicle rupture.
texts.t132: The interval between the onset of the LH surge and ovulation varies between women.
texts.t139: The main estrogens include estradiol, estrone, and estriol; in the reproductive age, estradiol is the primary one.
texts.t140: Estrogens regulate endometrial proliferation, cervical mucus properties, bone and vascular effects, and participate in feedback at the level of the HPO axis.
texts.t141: Estrogens are a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t142: Estrogens are not a single hormone, but a group of steroids. In the menstrual cycle, estradiol produced by the follicle plays a decisive role. Estrone becomes relatively more significant in peripheral tissue and after menopause, while estriol is of particular importance during pregnancy. Both their reproductive and systemic effects—on bone, skin, blood vessels, and the brain—are clinically important.

texts.t147: if bleeding is irregular and the endometrium is stimulated for a long time without progesterone -> consider chronic anovulation
texts.t148: Estrogens participate in endometrial proliferation and feedback along the HPO axis.
texts.t156: Estradiol: the main role in the cycle
texts.t158: Estradiol is the main estrogen of reproductive age and a key hormone of the follicular phase.
texts.t159: Estradiol is produced by the growing dominant follicle, stimulates endometrial proliferation, and initiates the LH peak when levels are sustained at a high level.
texts.t160: Estradiol is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t161: As the dominant follicle grows, estradiol production increases. Estradiol makes cervical mucus more permeable to sperm, enhances endometrial proliferation, and changes the type of feedback at the level of the pituitary and hypothalamus. This is why estradiol is one of the main 'conductors' of the first half of the cycle.
texts.t162: estradiol: main role in the cycle
texts.t164: Estradiol is synthesized by the dominant follicle and prepares the endometrium for proliferation.
texts.t165: Sustained high estradiol levels switch the feedback to positive and trigger the LH surge.
texts.t172: Progesterone is the main hormone of the luteal phase and secretory transformation of the endometrium.
texts.t173: After ovulation, progesterone is primarily synthesized by the corpus luteum and transitions the endometrium into the secretory phase.
texts.t174: Progesterone is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t175: Progesterone becomes the dominant hormone after ovulation. It makes the endometrium receptive to implantation, increases basal body temperature, decreases cervical mucus permeability, and changes the nature of feedback at the level of the hypothalamus and pituitary. Without normal ovulatory formation of the corpus luteum, a full progesterone phase will not occur.
texts.t176: Progesterone is interpreted relative to ovulation, rather than by a universal 'day 21'; a single test does not always describe the quality of the luteal phase.
texts.t179: Progesterone is a key hormone of the luteal phase and secretory transformation of the endometrium.
texts.t180: After ovulation, progesterone increases basal body temperature and thickens cervical mucus.
texts.t185: Prolactin is not a phase-specific hormone of the cycle, but its excess can suppress GnRH and disrupt ovulation.
texts.t186: With a persistent increase in prolactin, the activity of the GnRH pulse generator decreases, LH/FSH secretion drops, and oligomenorrhea, amenorrhea, and anovulation may occur.
texts.t187: Prolactin and the cycle is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t188: Prolactin is a pituitary hormone with a crucial role in lactation, but outside of pregnancy and breastfeeding, its excess often disrupts menstrual function. Prolactin levels are sensitive to sleep, stress, pain, physical activity, nipple stimulation, and certain medications. Therefore, prolactin assessment requires careful pre-analytical preparation.

texts.t209: Testosterone in women is present in small amounts and affects libido, bone tissue, and peripheral metabolism.
texts.t210: In women, testosterone is synthesized by the ovaries and adrenal glands, and its excess is primarily associated with hyperandrogenic states.
texts.t211: Testosterone and the female cycle — a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t212: Testosterone is not a 'foreign' hormone for the female body. It serves as a precursor to estrogens and participates in the regulation of sexual desire, muscle, and bone tissue. The clinical problem is usually associated not with its presence, but with an excess of the free fraction, which can lead to acne, hirsutism, and ovulation disorders.

texts.t231: Androgens in women: normal and excess
texts.t233: Androgens are also needed by the female body, but their excess disrupts ovulation and affects the skin, hair, and cycle.
texts.t234: Androgens include testosterone, androstenedione, DHEA, and DHEAS. Not only the total level but also the bioavailable fraction and accompanying symptoms are of clinical significance.
texts.t235: Androgens in women — a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t236: In physiological amounts, androgens participate in normal steroidogenesis and serve as a substrate for estrogen synthesis. In excess, they can inhibit the normal growth of the dominant follicle and contribute to chronic anovulation. They should be evaluated considering age, symptoms, SHBG, and possible sources — ovarian and adrenal.

texts.t244: Excess androgens in women are associated with hirsutism, acne, and anovulation.
texts.t252: AMH reflects the number of growing preantral and small antral follicles and is used as a marker of ovarian reserve.
texts.t253: AMH does not directly indicate egg quality and does not confirm fertility on its own. It helps assess the expected ovarian response to stimulation.
texts.t254: Anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t255: AMH is produced by the granulosa cells of preantral and small antral follicles. Its convenience lies in the fact that it is less dependent on the cycle day than FSH or inhibin B. However, AMH should be interpreted with caution: low AMH does not equate to immediate infertility, and high AMH does not guarantee normal ovulation. It is often elevated in PCOS and decreased when ovarian reserve is reduced.
texts.t256: Interpretation depends on the method, age, testing purpose, and laboratory; trends and context are more important than a single number.

texts.t263: AMH reflects the number of preantral and small antral follicles and serves as a marker of ovarian reserve.
texts.t264: AMH should not be used as the sole test for diagnosing PCOS or assessing fertility.
texts.t267: is not the primary cyclic feedback signal
texts.t268: AMH reflects ovarian reserve better than current fertility or egg quality; it should not be interpreted as a direct prediction of natural conception.
texts.t271: Inhibin B is produced by the granulosa cells of early antral follicles and suppresses FSH.
texts.t272: Inhibin B is important as part of the negative feedback in the early follicular phase. It is used less frequently than AMH because it is more variable throughout the cycle.
texts.t273: Inhibin B is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t274: Inhibin B helps regulate the growth of the follicular cohort by inhibiting FSH. In reproductive medicine, it is sometimes used as an additional marker of ovarian function, but in modern practice, it is inferior to AMH in terms of convenience and reproducibility for routine ovarian reserve assessment.
texts.t278: Inhibin B is produced by the granulosa cells of growing follicles and suppresses FSH.
texts.t279: Inhibin B is used less frequently than AMH in ovarian reserve assessment due to greater cyclic variability.

texts.t284: TSH itself does not regulate the cycle directly, but serves as an indicator of thyroid status, which affects ovulation and cycle regularity.
texts.t285: TSH abnormalities may accompany hypo- or hyperthyroidism, and these conditions are associated with cycle disturbances, changes in SHBG, prolactin, and ovulatory function.
texts.t286: TSH and the menstrual cycle — a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t287: TSH is useful as a screening marker for thyroid function in cases of cycle disturbances. Although it is not a reproductive hormone in the narrow sense, its alteration helps suspect an endocrine cause of anovulation, oligomenorrhea, or amenorrhea. Therefore, TSH is often included in the basic diagnostic workup.
texts.t288: Reference ranges depend on the method and clinical context; for reproductive assessment, TSH is interpreted alongside free T4 and clinical history.

texts.t307: Thyroid hormones support normal function of the ovaries, endometrium, and the metabolic environment for ovulation.
texts.t308: Both hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism can disrupt ovulation, lengthen or shorten the cycle, and cause anovulation.
texts.t309: Thyroid hormones and ovulation — a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t310: Thyroid hormones affect many tissues of the reproductive system, including the ovaries, endometrium, and placental structures. In hypothyroidism, an increase in TRH and secondary hyperprolactinemia is also possible, which increases the risk of anovulation. Therefore, in cases of cycle disturbances, it is important not only to measure TSH but also to evaluate the overall thyroid profile.
texts.t314: Both hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism can be accompanied by anovulation and cycle disturbances.
texts.t315: Assessment of thyroid function is recommended for amenorrhea and menstrual cycle disturbances.
texts.t321: Cortisol and the impact of stress on hormones
texts.t323: Chronic stress and HPA axis activation can suppress the reproductive axis.
texts.t324: Under severe or prolonged stress, CRH and cortisol levels increase, which can reduce GnRH pulsation, alter LH secretion, and disrupt ovulation.
texts.t325: Cortisol and the impact of stress on hormones is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t326: The impact of stress on the cycle is rarely limited to cortisol alone. It usually involves a combination of psycho-emotional load, sleep disturbances, energy deficiency, high physical activity, and changes in HPA axis function. Clinically, this may manifest as cycle prolongation, anovulation, or functional hypothalamic amenorrhea.

texts.t343: Insulin is closely linked to ovarian function and is particularly important in the pathogenesis of PCOS.
texts.t344: Hyperinsulinemia enhances ovarian androgen synthesis and reduces SHBG, thereby increasing free androgen levels.
texts.t345: Insulin and the reproductive system is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t346: Insulin is not only a metabolic hormone but also a reproductively significant one. In insulin resistance, the ovaries receive signals for increased androgenogenesis, and the liver synthesizes less SHBG. As a result, androgen bioavailability increases, ovulation is impaired, and the pathological cycle of PCOS is maintained.
texts.t350: Insulin affects the reproductive axis and ovarian steroidogenesis.
texts.t351: Insulin resistance plays a major role in the pathogenesis of PCOS and associated ovulatory disorders.
texts.t359: Leptin and its link to ovulation
texts.t361: Leptin signals the brain about the sufficiency of energy stores for reproduction.
texts.t362: With very low energy availability and low leptin, the reproductive axis may be inhibited; however, excess adipose tissue and leptin resistance can also be accompanied by disturbances.
texts.t363: Leptin and its link to ovulation is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t364: Leptin is an important mediator between energy status and reproduction. It does not control GnRH alone but is part of a signaling network that informs the brain whether there are sufficient resources to support ovulation. Therefore, both extreme thinness and obesity can disrupt the ovulatory cycle, although the mechanisms differ.
texts.t365: leptin and its link to ovulation
texts.t368: Leptin transmits information about energy stores and participates in the modulation of the GnRH network.
texts.t369: Both low energy availability and obesity can be accompanied by ovulatory dysfunction.
texts.t373: Why hormones change by cycle phases
texts.t375: Hormones change by phases because the cycle is built on a sequential shift between negative and positive feedback.
texts.t376: At the beginning of the cycle, low estradiol and progesterone allow FSH to rise slightly; then, the growing follicle increases estradiol and inhibin B, and after ovulation, progesterone predominates.
texts.t377: Why hormones change by cycle phases is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t378: The menstrual cycle is not a static set of levels but a dynamic system. First, the body recruits follicles, then selects a dominant follicle, prepares for ovulation, and after ovulation, remodels the endometrium under the influence of progesterone. Therefore, assessing hormones without specifying the cycle phase is often uninformative.

texts.t392: The LH peak is a pre-ovulatory hormonal surge, without which normal ovulation is impossible.
texts.t393: The pre-ovulatory LH peak occurs after a period of sustained high estradiol and triggers follicle rupture and ovulation.
texts.t394: The LH peak before ovulation is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t395: Commercial LH tests are used to identify the fertile window, but they do not provide an equally accurate prediction for everyone. There is inter-individual variability between the onset of the LH surge and ovulation; therefore, clinically, this peak is better interpreted as the approach of ovulation rather than its exact hour.

texts.t402: How the corpus luteum is formed
texts.t404: The corpus luteum is formed from the ovulated follicle following the LH surge.
texts.t405: After ovulation, granulosa and theca cells luteinize, vascular growth increases, and a temporary endocrine gland—the corpus luteum—is formed.
texts.t406: How the corpus luteum is formed—a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t407: The corpus luteum is a rapidly forming, highly vascularized structure capable of producing large amounts of progesterone. If implantation does not occur, it regresses. If pregnancy occurs, its function is maintained until the placenta begins to synthesize adequate progesterone.
texts.t408: how the corpus luteum is formed
texts.t410: The corpus luteum is formed from the ovulated follicle after the LH surge.
texts.t411: The primary function of the corpus luteum is progesterone synthesis.
texts.t417: The term "hormonal imbalance" is not a diagnosis, but a general description of a disruption in hormonal regulation.
texts.t418: Scientifically, it is more accurate to specify the particular disorder instead of using a general term: anovulation, hyperprolactinemia, hypothyroidism, hyperandrogenism, hypoestrogenism, etc.
texts.t419: What is hormonal imbalance—a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t420: In pop medicine, "hormonal imbalance" is often used to describe any complaint—from PMS to acne. In evidence-based clinical practice, this term is used only as a general phrase, followed by a clarification of the mechanism. This is important because treatment depends not on an abstract "imbalance," but on the specific cause and context.

texts.t430: Estrogen dominance: what is meant by this
texts.t432: The term "estrogen dominance" is widely used in popular literature but is not a standalone standard diagnosis in evidence-based gynecology.
texts.t433: In practice, it often conflates chronic anovulation, absence of the progesterone phase, obesity, perimenopause, or other specific conditions.
texts.t434: Estrogen dominance—a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t435: If a person has symptoms that popular sources call "estrogen dominance," it is clinically necessary to evaluate for chronic anovulation, irregular cycles, endometrial thickening, metabolic factors, perimenopause, or medication side effects. That is, it is important not to defend the term, but to translate it into diagnostically useful language.

texts.t451: Progesterone deficiency: when it is discussed
texts.t453: It is more accurate to speak of 'progesterone deficiency' only in the context of confirmed ovulation, the luteal phase, and the clinical objective.
texts.t454: In clinical practice, low progesterone is often associated not with an isolated deficiency, but with anovulation or incorrect timing of the test.
texts.t455: Progesterone deficiency is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t456: Since progesterone levels change drastically throughout the cycle, testing without reference to ovulation is often of little value. An important principle: first determine if ovulation occurred, then assess progesterone production in the mid-luteal phase. Otherwise, a false label of 'deficiency' may be applied when the actual problem is anovulation.

texts.t471: Hyperprolactinemia is one of the common endocrine causes of oligomenorrhea, amenorrhea, and anovulation.

texts.t486: Hypoestrogenism is a state of insufficient estrogenic effect on tissues.
texts.t487: It can manifest as vaginal dryness, decreased libido, hot flashes, sleep disturbances, amenorrhea, and bone loss.
texts.t488: Hypoestrogenism is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t489: Symptoms of hypoestrogenism depend on age, cause, and duration of the condition. In young age, this is particularly important because prolonged estrogen deficiency affects not only the cycle and fertility but also bone tissue, mucous membranes, and quality of life. Causes include hypothalamic amenorrhea, premature ovarian insufficiency, hyperprolactinemia, and certain medical interventions.

texts.t504: Hyperandrogenism manifests as acne, hirsutism, androgenic hair loss, and often ovulatory dysfunction.

texts.t506: Hyperandrogenism is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t507: Clinically, hyperandrogenism is not just a laboratory value but a symptomatic syndrome. Rapid development of virilization is a specific red flag requiring urgent evaluation.

texts.t524: Mood is influenced not only by absolute hormone levels but also by the rate of change, individual sensitivity, sleep quality, stress levels, and a history of mental health disorders. Therefore, the same cycle phase can feel different for different people. Scientifically, it is more accurate to speak not of a "bad phase," but of the interaction between biology and individual sensitivity.

texts.t543: How hormones affect skin, hair, and libido
texts.t545: Androgens have the strongest influence on acne, sebum, and hirsutism; estrogens and progesterone also change the state of the skin and sexual desire.
texts.t546: An increase in free androgens enhances sebum production and can cause acne and hirsutism, while pronounced hypoestrogenism is often associated with dryness and decreased libido.
texts.t547: How hormones affect skin, hair, and libido is a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t548: The condition of skin, hair, and libido is a multicomponent phenotype. It is influenced by hormones, genetics, receptor sensitivity, medications, the thyroid gland, stress, sleep quality, and relationships. Therefore, even with similar lab results, the clinical picture can differ between two people.

texts.t561: On which cycle days to test different hormones
texts.t563: The testing day depends on the goal: some hormones are evaluated in the early follicular phase, others after ovulation, and some can be tested on almost any day.
texts.t564: For FSH, LH, and estradiol, the early follicular phase is usually used, most often days 2–5 of the cycle. Progesterone is evaluated approximately 7 days after ovulation. AMH is relatively less dependent on the cycle day.
texts.t565: On which cycle days to test different hormones — a key element of hormonal regulation.
texts.t566: The most common mistake is trying to test all hormones on the same day and interpreting them the same way for everyone. For progesterone, timing relative to ovulation is critical, rather than a conventional "day 21". For prolactin, testing conditions are important. For TSH, the cycle phase is usually not significant. Therefore, a laboratory plan is always best built around the clinical question.

texts.t594: The menstrual cycle is a repeating hormonal process in which the brain, ovaries, and uterus coordinate follicle growth, ovulation, endometrial preparation, and menstruation.
texts.t595: The menstrual cycle is not just a period, but the functioning of the hypothalamus–pituitary–ovarian axis. GnRH pulses regulate FSH and LH, FSH supports follicle growth, the growing follicle produces estradiol, high estradiol triggers the LH peak, ovulation divides the cycle into two phases, and progesterone from the corpus luteum forms the luteal phase. If pregnancy does not occur, progesterone and estradiol levels drop, and menstruation begins.
texts.t596: The menstrual cycle is a cyclic neuroendocrine and reproductive system that ensures ovulation and preparation of the endometrium for possible implantation.
texts.t597: The menstrual cycle is regulated by the hypothalamus–pituitary–ovarian (HPO) axis. The hypothalamus releases GnRH in pulses; it is the frequency and amplitude of these pulses that determine the secretion of FSH and LH by the anterior pituitary gland. In the early follicular phase, FSH stimulates a cohort of antral follicles. One of them gains an advantage and becomes dominant. Its granulosa cells begin to actively synthesize estradiol and inhibin B. As estradiol levels rise, the endometrium enters the proliferative phase: glands lengthen, the stroma thickens, and the cervix produces mucus that is more permeable to sperm. When high estradiol levels are maintained long enough, negative feedback switches to positive feedback, triggering an LH peak. Ovulation usually occurs approximately 24–36 hours after the onset of the LH surge. After ovulation, the follicle transforms into the corpus luteum, which produces progesterone. Progesterone shifts the endometrium into the secretory phase, raises basal body temperature by approximately 0.3–0.5°C, and makes cervical mucus thicker. If pregnancy does not occur, the corpus luteum regresses, progesterone and estradiol levels drop, and the shedding of the functional layer of the endometrium — menstruation — begins. Clinically, it is important to note that menstrual-like bleeding does not always prove ovulation. In anovulatory cycles, bleeding can occur without a full luteal phase. Therefore, to interpret the cycle, one must evaluate not only the fact of bleeding but also cycle length, regularity, signs of ovulation, mucus characteristics, basal body temperature, and associated symptoms.

texts.t650: Why the body needs a menstrual cycle
texts.t652: The menstrual cycle is not only for pregnancy: it reflects the normal functioning of the reproductive and general neuroendocrine systems.
texts.t653: From a biological perspective, the cycle is necessary for egg maturation, ovulation, and preparation of the endometrium for potential implantation. From a clinical perspective, a regular ovulatory cycle is a marker of the normal functioning of the hypothalamic–pituitary–ovarian axis, sufficient energy availability, and the absence of significant endocrine dysregulation.
texts.t654: The menstrual cycle is a mechanism of reproductive readiness and simultaneously a biomarker of endocrine health.
texts.t655: The main biological task of the cycle is to prepare the body for pregnancy. In each cycle, a dominant follicle forms, the oocyte reaches maturity, ovulation occurs, and the endometrium transitions from the proliferative to the secretory phase. Without this sequence, natural conception is impossible. However, the significance of the cycle is broader. A regular ovulatory cycle reflects the coordinated work of the hypothalamus, pituitary gland, ovaries, thyroid gland, metabolic signals, and overall energy status. This is why clinicians use the cycle as an important health indicator. The disappearance or significant disruption of the cycle can be an early signal of hypothalamic dysfunction, energy deficiency, thyroid pathology, hyperprolactinemia, or PCOS. Estrogens and progesterone affect more than just the uterus. Estrogens are important for bone tissue, vascular tone, cognitive function, and mucous membranes. Progesterone affects thermoregulation, mood, and the secretory transformation of the endometrium. Therefore, persistent cycle disturbances affect not only fertility but also overall well-being.

texts.t707: How the cycle works: general overview
texts.t709: The cycle consists of the follicular phase, ovulation, and the luteal phase; these are controlled by the sequential changes in FSH, estradiol, LH, and progesterone.
texts.t710: At the beginning of the cycle, low estradiol and progesterone levels allow FSH to stimulate follicle growth. The dominant follicle produces estradiol; high estradiol levels trigger an LH surge, which initiates ovulation. After ovulation, the corpus luteum produces progesterone and forms the luteal phase. If pregnancy does not occur, progesterone levels drop, and menstruation begins.
texts.t711: The general cycle scheme is a sequential change of phases and feedback between the hypothalamus, pituitary gland, ovaries, and endometrium.
texts.t712: At the very beginning of the cycle, the corpus luteum from the previous cycle has already regressed, so progesterone and estradiol levels are low. This removes the inhibition on the pituitary gland, and FSH begins to support the growth of several antral follicles. As the follicles grow, they secrete estradiol and inhibin B, and one of them becomes dominant. When estradiol reaches a high, sustained level, the pituitary gland switches to positive feedback, and an LH surge occurs. It is the LH surge that triggers the final maturation of the oocyte, follicle rupture, and ovulation. After this, the follicle luteinizes, the corpus luteum forms, and the progesterone, or luteal, phase begins. Progesterone makes the endometrium secretory, increases basal body temperature, and decreases cervical mucus permeability. If the embryo does not implant, the corpus luteum regresses, progesterone and estradiol levels drop, and a new cycle begins. Clinically, it is important that cycle length is primarily determined by the duration of the follicular phase, not the luteal phase. Therefore, ovulation does not necessarily have to occur on day 14.

texts.t752: The average cycle length is about 28 days, but the clinically normal range in adults is wider: 24–38 days.
texts.t753: The number 28 days is an average, not a mandatory norm. In adults of reproductive age, a cycle length of 24–38 days is usually considered normal. Shorter or longer cycles more often require evaluation, especially if it is a new pattern.
texts.t754: Average cycle length is a mean value that does not replace the clinical normal range.
texts.t755: Although popular culture often describes the cycle as '28 days,' this is only a statistical average. In modern clinical interpretation, a normal cycle in adults usually falls within the range of 24–38 days. A cycle of 26, 31, or 35 days is not a pathology in itself if it is relatively stable and not accompanied by red flags. The reason for this variability is that the follicular phase is what changes most often from cycle to cycle. The luteal phase after ovulation is usually more stable in the same woman. Therefore, a longer cycle often means late ovulation rather than a mandatory pathology. It is clinically important to evaluate not only the length itself but also the context: age, regularity, presence of ovulatory signs, symptoms, and the suddenness of changes.

texts.t796: Small fluctuations in cycle length from month to month are acceptable. A normal cycle is determined not only by the number of days but also by relative regularity, probable ovulation, and the absence of alarming symptoms. Acceptable variability differs across different age periods.
texts.t797: Normal variations in cycle length are acceptable physiological fluctuations in the duration of the menstrual cycle without signs of pathology.
texts.t798: Clinical norm is not 'every cycle is the same,' but a reproducible physiological pattern. In adults of reproductive age, the cycle most often falls within the 24–38 day range, but even within this range, small natural fluctuations are possible. Modern interpretation relies not only on cycle length but also on variability between cycles. A small difference between months can be physiological because the follicular phase is sensitive to stress, sleep, travel, exercise, and energy status. According to clinical descriptions, regularity is assessed by the difference between the shortest and longest cycle, and acceptable fluctuations depend on age. It is important to remember: a cycle may formally fall within the normal range but require evaluation if it is accompanied by severe pain, abnormal bleeding, prolonged spotting, or absence of ovulation.

texts.t840: What to consider the first day of the cycle
texts.t842: Day 1 of the cycle is the first day of full menstrual bleeding.
texts.t843: Day 1 of the cycle is considered the first day of full menstrual bleeding, rather than occasional spotting 1–2 days prior. In practice, this is the day when bleeding corresponds to a normal period and requires standard hygiene products. This rule is important because the estimated follicular phase, timing for FSH, LH, and estradiol tests, and ovulation tracking benchmarks are all calculated from Day 1.
texts.t844: Clinical and digital cycle analytics require the same reference point every month. Therefore, the start of full bleeding is considered Day 1. Slight brown or pink spotting beforehand does not always reflect the true start of a new cycle: it may be residual endometrial shedding, intermenstrual spotting, a reaction to hormonal contraception, or bleeding for another reason. Why is this important? Because an incorrect cycle start shifts all calculations: the testing window for the early follicular phase, the estimated ovulation date, the interpretation of the basal body temperature chart, and the duration of the luteal phase. In tracking apps, this is particularly critical: if 'any trace of blood' is counted as Day 1, the algorithm may systematically be off by 1–2 days. If a person frequently confuses spotting with actual menstruation, it is helpful to additionally record the color, volume, need for a pad/tampon, presence of pain, and duration of the episode. If such episodes recur, especially alongside pain, post-coital bleeding, or bleeding between cycles, it is no longer a tracking issue but a reason for an in-person evaluation.

texts.t869: What are the phases of the menstrual cycle
texts.t871: The cycle is typically described through the menstrual, follicular, ovulatory, and luteal phases.
texts.t872: The cycle is conveniently described through four phases: menstrual, follicular, ovulatory, and luteal. At the ovarian level, the follicular phase, ovulation, and luteal phase are identified; at the uterine level, the menstrual, proliferative, and secretory phases. These schemes complement each other: the action of estradiol predominates in the first half of the cycle, and progesterone after ovulation.
texts.t873: The menstrual phase is the time when the functional layer of the endometrium is shed following a drop in progesterone and estradiol. Then the follicular phase begins: FSH stimulates follicle growth, granulosa cells produce estradiol, and the endometrium enters the proliferative phase and thickens again. At the same time, cervical mucus becomes wetter and more elastic. The ovulatory phase is a short interval around the release of the egg from the dominant follicle after the LH peak. The fertile window is formed around this phase. After ovulation, the cycle enters the luteal phase: the corpus luteum produces progesterone, the endometrium becomes secretory, basal body temperature usually rises, and cervical mucus thickens. For the user, knowing the phases helps understand why discharge, temperature, libido, breast sensitivity, and premenstrual symptoms change. For the physician, these phases are also important because various tests and instrumental studies are best interpreted on specific days of the cycle.
texts.t876: The follicular phase is the first part of the cycle, when follicles grow in the ovary and estrogen levels gradually increase.
texts.t877: The follicular phase begins on the first day of menstruation and ends with ovulation. During this period, FSH stimulates the growth of antral follicles, granulosa cells begin to actively synthesize 17-beta-estradiol and inhibin B, and one follicle gradually becomes dominant. It is the duration of the follicular phase that most often explains why an individual's total cycle may be 26, 29, or 33 days.
texts.t878: At the beginning of the follicular phase, progesterone and estradiol levels are low, so the inhibitory effect on the pituitary gland weakens and FSH rises moderately. Under the influence of FSH, several follicles begin to develop, but as estradiol and inhibin B levels rise, the FSH level decreases again. This creates selection: less sensitive follicles stop growing, while the dominant one continues to develop. Clinical reviews indicate that by approximately day 7 of the cycle, follicles of 9–10 mm are often visible, and the dominant follicle then grows by about 2 mm per day until it reaches an ovulatory size of 18–29 mm. At the uterine level, this corresponds to the proliferative phase: estradiol stimulates the restoration and thickening of the endometrium, increases the depth of the spiral arteries, and changes the composition of cervical mucus. The user may notice that after menstruation ends, mucus is initially sparse or sticky, and closer to ovulation, it becomes creamy, then clearer and more stretchy. If the follicular phase is excessively prolonged or ovulation does not occur, the cycle becomes longer or irregular. This can happen with stress, energy deficiency, PCOS, hyperprolactinemia, and the perimenopausal transition.
texts.t881: The ovulatory phase is a short period around the release of the egg from the ovary.
texts.t882: The ovulatory phase is a short period when the dominant follicle ruptures and releases an oocyte. The event is triggered by an LH surge, which occurs after sustained high levels of estradiol; ovulation typically occurs 36–44 hours after the onset of the LH surge. Immediately before it, cervical mucus becomes maximally watery, slippery, and stretchy, and after ovulation, these signs change rapidly.
texts.t883: By the end of the follicular phase, the dominant follicle reaches a mature size and produces high levels of 17-beta-estradiol. At a certain point, estradiol stops suppressing the pituitary gland and, instead, triggers positive feedback, leading to a sharp rise in LH and a smaller rise in FSH. Under the influence of this hormonal signal, proteases, prostaglandins, and other mediators are activated in the follicle, causing the follicular wall to thin and the oocyte to be released. For the user, ovulation is not always felt. Possible physical markers include brief unilateral lower abdominal pain, increased sexual desire, very clear and stretchy mucus, and sometimes minimal spotting. However, none of these signs guarantee ovulation 100%. Basal body temperature, conversely, rises only after ovulation, so it is better for confirming the fact of ovulation retrospectively. A deviation is considered a situation where ovulatory signs are consistently absent against a background of long or very irregular cycles, or there are symptoms of hyperandrogenism, galactorrhea, significant weight loss, or perimenopause. In such cases, one should consider an ovulation disorder rather than just late ovulation.
texts.t886: The luteal phase is the second half of the cycle after ovulation, when the corpus luteum produces progesterone.
texts.t887: The luteal phase begins after ovulation and lasts until the next menstruation. Its primary hormone is progesterone, which is produced by the corpus luteum under the influence of LH. Under the action of progesterone, the endometrium becomes secretory, basal body temperature typically rises by 0.3–0.6 °C, and cervical mucus thickens and becomes less permeable to sperm.
texts.t888: After ovulation, the wall of the ruptured follicle collapses, and the granulosa and theca cells luteinize to form the corpus luteum. This temporary endocrine structure begins to actively synthesize progesterone and, to a lesser extent, estradiol. Progesterone transitions the endometrium from a proliferative state to a secretory one: the glands become more coiled, accumulate glycogen, the vascular network becomes richer, and the tissue is better prepared for potential implantation. Physical markers of the second phase include a slight rise in morning temperature, a decrease in the amount of watery mucus, and sometimes increased breast sensitivity, sleepiness, or premenstrual symptoms. In most people, the duration of the luteal phase is more stable than the follicular phase and is often close to 12–14 days. If hCG does not appear, the corpus luteum regresses, progesterone drops rapidly, and this triggers menstruation. If the cycle is systematically short due to the second phase or if spotting begins quickly after ovulation, this is a reason not to self-diagnose but to discuss the situation with a doctor. An assessment of not only the phase duration but also confirmation of ovulation itself, the state of the endometrium, and other causes of bleeding is needed.
texts.t889: What happens to the ovaries during the cycle
texts.t891: In the ovaries, the growth of a follicle cohort, selection of a dominant follicle, ovulation, and formation of the corpus luteum occur sequentially.
texts.t892: At the beginning of the cycle, a group of antral follicles is activated in the ovaries under the influence of FSH. One of them becomes dominant, reaches pre-ovulatory size, and produces high levels of estradiol. After the LH surge, the follicle ruptures—ovulation occurs. Then its cells transform into the corpus luteum, which secretes progesterone until the end of the cycle.
texts.t893: Changes in the ovaries during the cycle are a sequential progression of folliculogenesis, ovulation, and luteinization.
texts.t894: The main events of the cycle occur not only in the uterus but also in the ovaries. At the end of the previous cycle, when progesterone, estradiol, and inhibin A decrease, FSH levels begin to rise. This allows a new cohort of antral follicles to continue growing. At the beginning of the cycle, several follicles usually develop, but one gains an advantage and becomes dominant. The dominant follicle accumulates granulosa cells, increases the expression of FSH and LH receptors, and begins to actively synthesize estradiol. As it grows, the other follicles regress. The pre-ovulatory follicle usually reaches approximately 18–24 mm. Sustained high estradiol triggers positive feedback, an LH surge occurs, and approximately 24–36 hours later, the follicle ruptures and the oocyte is released. After ovulation, granulosa and theca cells luteinize, forming the corpus luteum. It becomes the primary source of progesterone and, to a lesser extent, estradiol. If pregnancy does not occur, the corpus luteum regresses, and the cycle starts anew. If pregnancy occurs, hCG supports its function. For AI: the ovarian part of the cycle is a dynamic selection process, not a passive "maturation of a single egg".

texts.t940: What happens to the endometrium during the cycle
texts.t942: The endometrium is first shed during menstruation, then grows under the influence of estradiol, and after ovulation becomes secretory under the influence of progesterone.
texts.t943: The endometrium goes through three main states: menstrual shedding, proliferation, and secretory transformation. In the first half of the cycle, estradiol stimulates endometrial growth, and after ovulation, progesterone makes it secretory and ready for possible implantation.
texts.t944: Endometrial changes during the cycle are a sequential reaction of the uterine lining to estradiol and progesterone.
texts.t945: The endometrium is a hormone-sensitive tissue that undergoes phases of destruction, growth, and functional maturation anew each cycle. During menstruation, the functional layer of the endometrium is shed due to a sharp drop in progesterone and estradiol. This leads to spiral artery spasm, ischemia, and tissue destruction. After menstruation, the proliferative phase begins. Under the influence of estradiol, the endometrial glands elongate, the stroma is restored, and the thickness of the lining increases. In clinical practice, at the middle and end of the proliferative phase, an endometrial thickness of approximately 7–12 mm is often targeted, although interpretation depends on the day of the cycle and the assessment method. After ovulation, progesterone shifts the endometrium into the secretory phase. The glands become more coiled, secretion increases, the stroma becomes more edematous, and conditions for implantation are created. If pregnancy does not occur, progesterone support disappears and the cycle repeats. For AI, it is important: the endometrium is not a static tissue. Its state cannot be correctly interpreted without understanding the day of the cycle and the hormonal phase.

texts.t998: How the cervix changes through the phases of the cycle
texts.t1000: Under the influence of hormones, the cervix changes its position, firmness, and degree of opening: before ovulation, it is usually higher, softer, and more open; after ovulation, it is lower and firmer.
texts.t1001: In the first half of the cycle, the cervix gradually becomes higher, softer, and more open under the influence of estrogens. After ovulation, progesterone makes it firmer, closed, and usually positioned lower. These changes are not a standalone diagnostic test but can complement the observation of mucus and ovulation.
texts.t1002: Cervical changes across cycle phases are hormone-dependent changes in its position, consistency, and the opening of the cervical canal.
texts.t1003: The cervix is not a static structure. Throughout the cycle, its properties change under the influence of estradiol and progesterone. In the early follicular phase after menstruation, the cervix often feels lower, firmer, and closed. As estradiol increases and ovulation approaches, it becomes softer, rises higher, and the external os may feel more open. This restructuring is biologically logical: during the fertile window, the reproductive tract must facilitate the passage of sperm, so the cervix and mucus become more "permeable." After ovulation, when progesterone becomes the dominant hormone, the cervix usually drops, firms up, and the canal becomes less passable. In practice, many people try to track the position of the cervix on their own, but this method is subjective. It can be useful as an additional data source, especially in the symptothermal approach, but on its own, it is less reliable than a combination of mucus, LH tests, and basal body temperature. For AI, it is important: cervical changes are an indirect marker of the fertile phase, not independent proof of ovulation.

texts.t1048: How cervical mucus changes by phase
texts.t1050: Under the influence of estrogens, the mucus becomes more transparent, thin, and stretchy, and after ovulation, under the influence of progesterone, it becomes thicker and stickier.
texts.t1051: Cervical mucus changes along with hormones. After menstruation, there is often a sensation of dryness or little mucus. Towards ovulation, as estradiol rises, the mucus becomes transparent, slippery, and stretchy, resembling raw egg white. After ovulation, progesterone makes it thicker, cloudy, and less permeable to sperm.
texts.t1052: Changes in cervical mucus across cycle phases are hormone-dependent alterations of cervical secretion related to fertility.
texts.t1053: Cervical mucus is one of the most useful clinical and behavioral markers of fertility. In the early follicular phase after menstruation, there may be little mucus, and subjectively, dryness is often felt. As estradiol increases, the cervix begins to produce more transparent, thin, and elastic mucus. It is during this period that sperm find an optimal environment for survival and progression. In the pre-ovulatory days, the mucus is often described as "egg white": it is transparent, slippery, and stretches between the fingers. This reflects the estrogen-dominant phase and the proximity of ovulation. After ovulation, progesterone changes the properties of the secretion: the mucus becomes thicker, stickier, cloudier, and less conducive to sperm. Sometimes, a sensation of dryness subjectively recurs. For the AI, it is important: cervical mucus is an early indicator of the fertile window that appears before ovulation is confirmed by temperature. However, changes in mucus can be distorted by vaginal infections, spermicides, certain medications, dehydration, and individual characteristics.

texts.t1101: How basal body temperature changes by phase
texts.t1103: Before ovulation, basal body temperature is usually lower, and after ovulation, it rises under the influence of progesterone and remains higher until the end of the cycle.
texts.t1104: In the follicular phase, basal body temperature is usually lower. After ovulation, when the corpus luteum begins to secrete progesterone, the temperature rises by approximately 0.3–0.5°C and remains higher throughout the luteal phase. Therefore, BBT helps confirm that ovulation has already occurred but does not predict it in advance.
texts.t1105: The change in basal body temperature by phase is a thermoregulatory effect of progesterone, reflecting the transition from the follicular to the luteal phase.
texts.t1106: Basal body temperature (BBT) is the temperature at complete rest, measured immediately upon waking before any activity. In the first half of the cycle, before ovulation, the temperature is usually lower. After ovulation, progesterone produced by the corpus luteum acts on the hypothalamic thermoregulatory centers and raises the basal temperature. Classically, a rise of about 0.3–0.5°C is expected, sometimes described as 0.5–1°F. This rise usually persists throughout the luteal phase and drops before menstruation when progesterone falls. If the elevation persists for 18 days or more, it may indirectly indicate pregnancy, but it does not replace a test. Clinically, BBT is primarily useful as a retrospective confirmation of ovulation rather than a tool for precise prediction of the day. The chart is affected by lack of sleep, alcohol, illness, changes in measurement time, flights, and certain medications. Important for AI: BBT increases accuracy when combined with LH tests and mucus, but is limited on its own.

texts.t1159: Cycle regularity depends on the coordinated function of the hypothalamus, pituitary gland, ovaries, metabolic signals, thyroid gland, prolactin, stress levels, and energy availability.
texts.t1160: The cycle remains regular when ovulation occurs predictably. This is influenced by GnRH pulsation, FSH and LH levels, ovarian function, prolactin, thyroid hormones, stress, sleep, nutrition, body weight, physical activity, illness, and certain medications. Most often, when regularity is disrupted, the follicular phase is affected, causing the cycle to lengthen or become chaotic.
texts.t1161: Cycle regularity is the relative repeatability of cycle length and phases, depending on stable ovulatory function and intact neuroendocrine regulation.
texts.t1162: A regular cycle is not an "ideally identical" number of days every month, but a reproducible ovulatory pattern without significant deviations. For the cycle to remain regular, the entire hypothalamus–pituitary–ovarian axis must function: the hypothalamus must maintain normal GnRH pulsation, the pituitary must secrete FSH and LH, and the ovaries must respond with follicle growth, ovulation, and the production of estradiol and progesterone. Regularity is influenced by several major groups of factors. The first is endocrine regulation: thyroid dysfunction, hyperprolactinemia, decreased ovarian reserve, PCOS, or hypothalamic dysfunction can alter ovulation. The second is metabolic and energetic: energy deficiency, rapid weight gain or loss, low leptin, intense training, and RED-S can suppress GnRH and make ovulation rare or absent. The third is behavioral and external: lack of sleep, stress, flights, changes in routine, and certain medications. Why does the cycle most often lengthen in practice? Because the follicular phase is more sensitive to external and internal influences than the luteal phase. If ovulation is delayed, the entire cycle becomes longer. Therefore, regularity is essentially a clinical marker of stable ovulatory function and overall neuroendocrine health.

texts.t1202: When a cycle is considered regular
texts.t1204: A cycle is considered regular when its length does not change chaotically, and the variation between cycles remains within the age-appropriate norm.
texts.t1205: A regular cycle is not necessarily a cycle of the same length every month. An adult cycle is considered regular if the cycle length changes moderately and predictably. Modern clinical guidelines use an acceptable variation between the shortest and longest cycle: usually up to 7 days for ages 26–41 and up to 9 days for ages 18–25 and 42–45.
texts.t1206: A regular cycle is a cycle with physiologically acceptable inter-cycle variability and maintained predictability.
texts.t1207: Many consider a cycle regular only if it arrives "exactly every 28 days." This is incorrect. In modern gynecological interpretation, regularity is defined not by absolute sameness, but by an acceptable variation between cycles. If the cycle changes by a few days but the pattern remains reproducible, it usually corresponds to the norm. Age matters. At ages 26–41, a cycle is usually considered regular if the variation between the shortest and longest cycle does not exceed approximately 7 days. At ages 18–25 and 42–45, a larger variation—up to 9 days—is acceptable. This reflects physiological characteristics: in late adolescence/early adulthood and late reproductive age, ovulation may be less stable. Regularity is important as an indirect marker of ovulatory function but does not automatically prove ovulation. In some individuals, bleeding can be quite predictable even with impaired ovulation. Therefore, regularity is a strong but not absolute sign of a normal cycle.

texts.t1243: When a cycle is considered irregular
texts.t1245: A cycle is considered irregular when cycle length varies excessively, bleeding becomes unpredictable, or the cycle falls outside normal age-related boundaries.
texts.t1246: An irregular cycle is not just one unusual month. Irregularity is typically defined when the cycle loses predictability, the variation between cycles exceeds age-related guidelines, or bleeding occurs too infrequently, too frequently, or chaotically. An adult cycle that consistently falls outside the 24–38 day range often requires evaluation.
texts.t1247: An irregular cycle is a cycle with excessive inter-cycle variability, loss of predictability, or deviation from the age-related physiological norm.
texts.t1248: Irregularity is a clinical pattern, not a diagnosis. A cycle is considered irregular if it becomes excessively variable, if bleeding occurs unpredictably, or if cycle lengths systematically fall outside age-related normal ranges. In clinical practice, this often appears as an alternation of short, long, and skipped cycles. Irregularity is particularly important because it often reflects ovulatory dysfunction. If the follicular phase either lengthens or ovulation does not occur at all, the menstrual pattern becomes chaotic. Causes may be physiological during adolescence and perimenopause, but require closer attention during the stable reproductive years. For AI: irregularity is an entry point for differential thinking. Age, pattern duration, signs of ovulation, body weight, stress, medications, and endocrine symptoms must be considered.

texts.t1288: Small fluctuations in cycle length from month to month are acceptable and often reflect follicular phase variability.
texts.t1289: A normal cycle does not have to be the same length every month. A difference of a few days can be physiological, especially if the cycle remains predictable, ovulatory signs persist, and there are no alarming symptoms. Small fluctuations are most often related to the fact that ovulation does not always occur on the same day.
texts.t1290: Normal cycle fluctuations are physiological inter-cycle variability without signs of pathology.
texts.t1291: Even in a healthy person, the cycle can change slightly from month to month. This is because the follicular phase—the part of the cycle that lasts until ovulation—is sensitive to stress, sleep, physical exertion, travel, energy status, and random biological fluctuations. Therefore, one cycle might be 28 days, another 31, and a third 29, and this can still be a normal variant. The key criterion is not absolute uniformity, but the maintenance of overall predictability and the absence of red flags. If ovulation simply shifts slightly, the entire cycle will lengthen or shorten slightly, but this does not indicate pathology. Conversely, a chaotic loss of structure, very wide variation, or a combination with pain, abnormal bleeding, or infertility makes the picture less physiological. For AI, it is important: the cycle should be evaluated over a series of cycles, not just one month.

texts.t1333: In the first years after menarche, the cycle is often more variable and anovulatory because the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis is still maturing.
texts.t1334: In adolescents, the cycle is often longer and less regular than in adults. In the first 1–3 years after menarche, ovulation may not occur in every cycle because the GnRH–FSH–LH axis is still reaching full maturity. However, excessively long intervals, very heavy bleeding, or persistent irregularity several years after menarche require evaluation.
texts.t1335: The adolescent cycle is the menstrual pattern of the first years after menarche, characterized by increased variability due to the immaturity of neuroendocrine regulation.
texts.t1336: After menarche, the cycle does not become adult-like and fully ovulatory instantly. In the first years, the hypothalamus does not always produce GnRH with the stable pulsation required for regular ovulation. Therefore, adolescent cycles are more often anovulatory, longer, and less predictable. This is physiological to a certain extent. A greater variation in cycle length than in adults is acceptable, and occasional long cycles do not necessarily indicate pathology. However, it is clinically important to monitor the boundaries: very long intervals between periods, heavy bleeding, severe pain, anemia, and persistent irregularity continuing several years after menarche should no longer be automatically attributed to age. For AI: greater variability is acceptable in adolescents, but this does not eliminate the need to recognize red flags.
